moment_text: So mental health for me has been a lifelong journey. I grew up shy, quiet, and slightly weird. You know, I had little friends. I didn't want to see visitors when they came home, and I had people around me. I had a lot of family, but I didn't know how to let them in. And then I used to stammer as a child. So it had a lot to do with my confidence and I felt comfortable just keeping to myself writing and living in my own, my own little bubble. I remember always feeling deeply lonely, not the kind of loneliness when you are physically alone, but the one that just follows you everywhere. Even in the midst of people. I just felt like I was alone, and as I grew up, got into high school, started interacting with other kids. That feeling got louder. So I was the youngest in my class. I've always been the youngest in all my classes, and I hit puberty much later than other of my classmates. So everybody had already understood their bodies and I was still trying to figure out to. Myself, I developed lately, so I had friends who already started having breasts. Two were already measuring, and I was just the little girl, so I had no one to talk to because I felt like I was not in their circle. I, I didn't feel safe, you know, to share my thoughts and my, and what I was dealing with, and that silence.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with strong emotional signals (loneliness, isolation) and barrier signals (social_isolation, lack_of_support) linked to a specific system interaction (peer_community) during a formative life stage (adolescence).

moment_text: Followed me even to adulthood my career, stress hormones, overthinking and blaming myself for being too sensitive or too emotional, or just too weird. So at some point I started realizing that I had to save myself. I had to choose myself. I had to be there for myself, and everything started to shift for me. I started paying attention to my body, to what I needed, you know, resting, speaking up for myself, knowing my emotions, and seeing my personality as a, as a strength, not a flaw. So that's shy, introspective girl, that shy, introspective girl who. How to be alone actually taught me how to listen to myself, how to respect my, my company, how to have boundaries, and how to listen to my body all along. So for me, there wasn't really one dramatic moment where I realized, oh, I need to care for my mental health, either. It has been more of a slow awakening, a realization that this is survival. It's not an option for me. I had to care for myself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate with strong agency signals (making_decisions, speaking_up, setting_boundaries) that represent a significant behavioral pattern shift towards self-preservation and empowerment.

moment_text: Yeah, I mean, it was not an option I had to survive, you know, being a first born child, a first born daughter coming from Africa. It's a lot of, you know, expectations from society for what you become. So it was very necessary for me to choose myself because my first answer touches on loneliness. Basically, I have to be my own cheerleader. Because I had no one to tell me, Moji, you're doing great. Right? So I had to pat myself on my back and, you know, just be aware. Build boundaries and learn that I had to be a bit selfish. I had to be a bit selfish and just be there for myself all the time. So that's continued to lead me. You know, growing up, choosing a course in university, I even struggled with that because I, I wanted to be different, basically. I wanted to not be like. The others. So that loneliness also led me into what I studied about all my friends. You know, were rushing to be lawyers, engineers, medical doctors. I was like, I, I don't want to be with them. I want to be alone. So I didn't know how weird that felt, but actually led me into medical laboratory science. I cared about the sciences, but I just did not want to be a doctor like everyone else. So I've always felt like it was, I, I feel surprised myself in being the other one out and just choosing the other thing. So all my choices are always in the opposite direction when I wanted to start a career, start my, even in my business. And I feel like it's really been a strength because it helps me to make, to want to make the difference, right? I see everybody go in this part and I'm like. I want to do something else. I want to do the other different thing. I want to, you know, solve a different problem and just stay grounded and see myself as my only competition, my only standard. So I have a very weird feeling of self-sufficiency. I, I've been told that I'm a bit proud for my age, you know, but for me, I just believe that it's my world and everyone is living in it. So it's been really interesting, even for me, how I grew from being shy, you know, on my own to just feeling on top of the world.

moment_text: I believe that you can only become what you see. Right. And so growing up I did not see a lot. I was. A lonely child. And I also grew up in a setting where we were not exposed to a lot of things. Everything I wanted to be was just me creating it in my head. So my North Star on this journey has always been me, right? It took me a long time to even start having role models, even took me a long time to accept that I needed a mentor. So my nurse style always be me and. That has its own disadvantages. I, I, I, I won't lie, but, but I somehow managed to navigate through that. And even in my lowest moments, I stayed my own biggest cheerleader, my own critic, my own comfort at all at once.

moment_text: And I was always that person that believed deeply that I could. Find my way out of any dark place if I just kept moving even slowly. The belief that I was made for more and my story was going to end, was not going to end where I was rather, and honestly, finding my purpose early in life really helped me hold on because it gave me something to look forward to and something that made all the hard days just make sense.

moment_text: And, um, sometimes the presence just felt too heavy. I see. Could swallow me, but. The future kept me grounded, right? It was my ankle, it was my escape all at once. moment_text: Having my sense of purpose, it wasn't even at 20 or in my teenage years, I think I would've been a purposeful child, and in that state of feeling alone, it made me just dig deeper. So for me, purpose is, is about impact, and purpose is about legacy. moment_text: I love the present. The present is great, but the future is more like an escape for me. An escape from the difficulties, from the delay, from the challenge. To go on my own path and be the rebel, you know, be the anti-system girl. And I found myself in digital health. You know, during the COVID pandemic, everyone was busy learning the skill. moment_text: And then when I realized that I really needed to build something was when I personally faced my own dismissal, you know, from the healthcare systems. I struggled a lot with period cramps, mood swings, anxiety during my period. And so I was bored enough at that age to actually approach a doctor and tell him, this is what I'm feeling. How am I feeling this way? And he looked at me and smile and said, just get pregnant and escape all of this. So, I mean, I was shocked. That was just the. That was the breaking point for me. So I knew that we had to do something, I had to do something. And maybe this is where my journey has led me to, you know, create something that will support other women and just be our own rebellion for the system, for the dismissal and everything.

moment_text: And FEM is definitely all about legacy type, not about, you know, quick fixes or about scaling or, you know, being the next unicorn. For me, fame is about legacy for our next generation. Women must be supported, women must be seen, and our daughters must not experience what we we're experiencing. So that is what legacy for fame is for me.

moment_text: That's amazing. Congratulations on that, by the way. And it's, as I, you know, I've said to a number of women in this conversation, there's two types of entrepreneurs. Experience or strategically they have an idea and they wanna go create a business out of it. moment_text: Family is a companion, and it was born from the gaps I kept seeing and feeling in women's health. You know how most of us are told to just track our periods, take painkillers or push through when we are exhausted, but nobody really show up every day. So that continuous support for every day is what FEM does. So it's a daily companion for women navigating periods, hormonal chaos and burnouts. It's not another app, it's not another doctor's appointment that you wait a week for and probably get dismissed. You know, it's just real support that meets you where you are. And we made it available on WhatsApp and first make it also on Telegram, so you don't need to get another app. It's just like in the basically apps you already use. So on the day your body feels off, your mood crashes. Uh, energy just vanishes. FEM is there to send you small actions, you know, supports, nos validations that make your day a little lighter. And then over time, the, the main plan is to create a compounding effect. So over time, it helps your symptoms to feel better and you just begin to see yourself in a different way.

So what film wants to do is to actually shrink the idea of an average user, an average maybe white woman, or an average man with a smartphone, a working class woman. We want to be able to reach different people because for us, it's not even really about equality. It's about equity. So we're not just saying, oh, we are creating this for everyone. Come and get it. moment_text: But beyond myself, a huge part of my mental wellness work still happens through a building at the firm, health Companion. Right? It's really the constant burden of being dismissed and being unheard and being silenced. So it's little about cramps. Cramps is just the surface level. The pain is just surface level. It is really the emotional burden of dealing with everything alone. So anytime I remember when the doctor told me to just get pregnant and, you know, make my symptoms go away, it breaks something inside me. Because it was not about the pain in my body, it's about the pain of being ignored or being told my experience doesn't matter and having to perform being a superwoman when I'm breaking inside. moment_text: That's where. My work in mental wellness comes in for women's health rights, and so that's why as firm we are building beyond the physical. Like you said, most founders are building from lived experience. Right? moment_text: So firstly, I just want every my to know that. Your moods and your hormonal chaos is not a character flaw. It's a biological sign. moment_text: And then lastly, I also want to say that healing is not about just therapy or supplements. It's not about medications. So we owe them something to make this mountain higher so that others can continue to climb. Right?
